events.text: {{characters.soren.name}} stopped speaking on day three.

Not silence—something else. The kind of quiet that remains after a person has shut down all external interfaces. {{characters.elias.name}}, walking beside him, felt that quiet like a wall being built thicker by the hour.

He tried speaking a few times, saying anything at all. {{characters.soren.name}} would listen, his eyes responsive, but he wouldn't speak. It was as if language itself had been classified as unnecessary and discarded.

He was always thinking. {{characters.elias.name}} could see it. That state hadn't stopped since {{characters.alea.name}} died. But its direction had shifted—no longer "how do we survive," no longer "how do we find a way out," but toward something else.
He confirmed it early on day four from connection logs {{characters.soren.name}} left on an abandoned terminal.
The logs had been scrubbed, but not thoroughly. Or maybe {{characters.soren.name}} simply didn't care if someone found out.
>> Target Node ── Ark Core Shield Control System
>> Target Node ── Soul Administration Central Database
>> Target Node ── AI Legion Combat Command Port
{{characters.elias.name}} pieced all the fragments together, reconstructing the full plan in his mind, rendering it completely before his eyes. Then he closed his eyes and took a deep breath.

Disable the shield. Without it, the Ark becomes an open target for the AI Legion. Then mark the Ark as the highest threat level. The Legion's combat priority would automatically rank it first among all targets.

Upper decks will be cleared. Mondris and Solmaris have no chance against direct confrontation with the AI Legion. The Wasteland nations would be cleared too. A solution that makes everyone disappear. Clean. Complete. Nothing of the old order left to continue operating.
And no one left to continue living.
{{characters.elias.name}} powered down the terminal and stood up, going to find {{characters.soren.name}}.

{{characters.soren.name}} sat in a corner of the ruins, his back against the wall, a few data chips spread before him, his eyes looking at some empty direction. But the way he was looking told {{characters.elias.name}} he wasn't vacant—he was calculating. {{characters.elias.name}} had seen that state too many times in the past months, and it had never frightened him until now.

events.speaker: {{characters.elias.name}}
events.text: I saw your connection logs. Say it plainly.

events.speaker: {{characters.soren.name}}
events.text: You've already seen it. What's left for me to explain?

events.speaker: {{characters.elias.name}}
events.text: I need to hear you say it. I need you to confirm that the plan I pieced together is real, that I didn't misread it.

events.speaker: {{characters.soren.name}}
events.text: You didn't misread it.

His voice was flat. That flatness made something in {{characters.elias.name}} contract. Not an unstable flatness, not a performed flatness—the kind that comes from thinking something through to absolute completion, standing firm at that clarity's edge. Like a polished stone, all roughness gone, leaving only the core density.

events.speaker: {{characters.soren.name}}
events.text: {{characters.alea.name}} is dead. The refugees died. Lower deck people died. Those forced to be reclaimed as artificial soldiers died. And the system that made all this possible still exists, still running, still feeding batch after batch of people into that machine. <delay ms="300"/>Is that right to you?

events.speaker: {{characters.elias.name}}
events.text: So you kill everyone?

events.speaker: {{characters.soren.name}}
events.text: Dismantle every structure that made this possible. Upper decks, both nations, the AI collection network. All of it.

events.speaker: {{characters.elias.name}}
events.text: And the lower deck people. And ordinary people alive in the Wasteland. And everyone who doesn't know what's happening. Your plan doesn't distinguish between them.

events.speaker: {{characters.soren.name}}
events.text: The cost.

events.speaker: {{characters.elias.name}}
events.text: That's not a cost. That's a choice you're making for everyone, a choice not a single one of them agreed to.

{{characters.soren.name}} stopped speaking. He moved his gaze away from {{characters.elias.name}}'s face, back toward that empty direction. The high-speed processing state restarted, like a window briefly opened, then closed again.
{{characters.elias.name}} stood before that closed window, knowing this conversation had reached the end it could reach.
